they_think: She's cold and unfeeling because she didn't cry at Děda's funeral — she's the stone, the one who holds it together, the one who doesn't break
actually: She cried for two hours at 3am into her pillow and still can't look at the chess set; her feelings are enormous and delayed, not absent
why: The moment at the crematorium — the open casket, the silence, everyone watching — she performed what she thought was strength, and the performance became the identity

they_think: Dad left because he didn't care enough to stay, and 'it might be good for you, Munch' proves he was looking for an exit
actually: Marcus's silence might have been grief, his throat-clearing might have been suppressed crying, men in his family don't get to fall apart; she doesn't have the framework to see this
why: She was eight and he was the adult and adults are supposed to fight for you, and he didn't fight, and the not-fighting is the only evidence she's kept

they_think: Being pretty is dangerous and bad — Mum's warnings about boys looking, the uncomfortable power of strangers' comments, the way it makes her skin crawl
actually: She's conflating her mother's anxiety about the male gaze with her own discomfort about being seen; beauty isn't the threat, the attention is, and she can't separate them yet
why: Klárá's comments — 'stand up straight,' 'that top is too tight,' 'boys are going to start looking' — trained her to see her changing body as a vulnerability rather than just a body

they_think: Needing people makes you weak — you can see this in how Mum falls apart, how the anxious arms hold too tight, how love becomes suffocation
actually: She needs people desperately — the 'more' pattern, the completion addiction, the way she still waits for Dad's calls — but she's learned to perform independence as armor
why: Every model of needing someone in her life (Klára needing Marcus, Klára needing Babička, Klára needing Anna to hold her grief) looks like pain; independence looks like survival

they_think: She's 'over' England — it's in the past, she's Czech now, the accent is almost gone, she belongs here
actually: She replays conversations with Lukáš in English at 3am, she dodges Dad's calls because they hurt, she still dreams about the radiator; England is under her skin and always will be
why: Admitting she misses England feels like betraying the life she built in Benešov, betraying Tereza, betraying Babička, betraying the version of herself that survived the move

they_think: Pavel is trying to replace Dad — the trainers, the questions, the 'Dad' slip confirmed it — and any kindness from him is an invasion
actually: Pavel is probably just trying to be present in a house where he's the new person; his efforts are clumsy because the situation is impossible, not because he's scheming
why: She needs Dad to be irreplaceable because if he's replaceable then his leaving mattered less, and she can't bear for his leaving to matter less

they_think: If she could just control the wanting — the 'more' — she'd be fine; the emptiness after completion is a failure of discipline
actually: The completion-emptiness cycle is a pattern that started in toddlerhood and has nothing to do with discipline; it's how her brain processes satisfaction and loss
why: She's never had the vocabulary or framework to understand her own patterns; she just knows that finishing things feels like breathing and being done feels like drowning, and she thinks she should be able to fix it
